title: The Incarnation and Irresistible Grace
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Incarnation and Irresistible Grace (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Continuing to consider the grace of God revealed in the incarnation
John 6:35: I am the bread of life — whoever comes will never hunger
All that the Father gives to Christ will come to Him (v.37)
Everyone who comes, Christ will not cast out (v.37)
Christ came to do the Father’s will: lose nothing given to Him (v.39)
The incarnation as the means of irresistible grace
summary: An Advent sermon connecting the incarnation of Christ to the doctrine of irresistible (effectual) grace. Expounding John 6:35-40, the sermon shows that Christ’s coming in the flesh is the instrument through which all the Father has given to the Son are effectually drawn and kept. The incarnation is not merely a historical event but the very means by which God’s sovereign grace is applied to the elect.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading — John 6:35–40

> *"And Jesus said to them, 'I am the bread of life. He who comes to me shall never hunger, and he who believes in me shall never thirst. But I said to you that you have seen me and yet do not believe. All that the Father gives me will come to me, and the one who comes to me I will by no means cast out. For I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will, but the will of him who sent me. This is the will of the Father who sent me, that of all he has given me I should lose nothing, but should raise it up at the last day. And this is the will of him who sent me, that everyone who sees the Son and believes in him may have everlasting life; and I will raise him up at the last day.'"*

## The Grace Revealed in the Incarnation Is Powerful

This morning we saw that the Word became flesh, full of grace and truth. This afternoon we want to see that the grace of God revealed in Jesus Christ is not only real — it is powerful. It is, in fact, all-powerful. It is as powerful as the God who bestows it.

We all like to be in control. We struggle with anxiety precisely because anxiety is the desire to control or know the future — and we cannot. The Scriptures teach that we are to be dependent on God for everything, that he is Almighty.

Now, does anyone here doubt that God is Almighty? No one who professes faith in Christ will deny that. Yet when we try to make God's omnipotence *personal*, we often resist. God is sovereign over your life? When he wants to save someone — and that person says "no"? We are saying that God is not Almighty when we say we can resist his saving grace.

The truth is that **the grace of God cannot be resisted**.

## What Is Grace?

The most common definition: grace is unmerited favor — something we do not deserve. But more precisely, grace is **God's disposition toward us** — his loving approval of us because of the Lord Jesus Christ, which strengthens us to obey him. When we pray for God's grace, we are praying that God would look favorably upon us so that we might obey him.

The Reformed tradition teaches that this grace, in the matter of salvation, is *irresistible*. When God has chosen someone to be saved and sends his Spirit to change that person from hateful to loving, no one can resist him.

When God says, "I am going to save this person," that person cannot ultimately say "no." God does not say, "I really wanted to save Johnny, but I respect his will — he told me no, so I am powerless." When God chooses to be favorable to someone, that person cannot do anything but come to faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.

## God Does Not Drag Anyone Kicking and Screaming

This does not mean God drags people into the kingdom against their wills. None of us came to Christ kicking and screaming. We came willingly — because God *changed our hearts*.

Instead of overpowering the will, God changes what controls the will — that is, the heart. The will is like a machine: what goes in determines what comes out. God changes the nature, changes the heart, so that the will now willingly chooses God. Man is truly sorry for his sin and genuinely loves God — because he has been given a new heart.

This is what theologians call **effectual calling** or **irresistible grace**. It emphasizes the power of this saving work, distinguishing it from the *general call* of the gospel.

## The General Call and the Effectual Call

God calls us to give the gospel to everyone:

> *"Come now, and let us reason together."* (Isaiah 1:18)

> *"Look to me, and be saved, all you ends of the earth!"* (Isaiah 45:22)

> *"Come to me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest."* (Matthew 11:28)

> *"If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink."* (John 7:37)

> *"Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature."* (Mark 16:15)

This is the general call — issued to all, without exception. And we are called to proclaim it to everyone.

But not everyone who hears the gospel comes to faith. We all know that from experience. However, those who do come, only come because they heard the gospel — because the Holy Spirit used that proclamation to effectively call the elect and change their hearts.

> *"All that the Father gives me will come to me, and the one who comes to me I will by no means cast out."* (John 6:37)

This is spoken as a reality, not a potentiality. Not "might come" but "will come." The Father's gift is certain; the coming is certain.

> *"No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him."* (John 6:44)

No one comes on his own. The Father draws him.

Acts 16:14 records Lydia's conversion:

> *"The Lord opened her heart to heed the things spoken by Paul."*

It was the Lord who opened the heart. Lydia did not open her own heart by sheer willpower. When God opens a heart, that person understands, believes, and comes.

In Acts 26, Paul recounts what Jesus told him he would do: "I will deliver you from the Jewish people, as well as from the Gentiles, to whom I now send you, to open their eyes, in order to turn them from darkness to light."

## Regeneration Precedes Faith

Ephesians 2 makes the logic plain. Paul says in verse 4–5: "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ." How much believing does a dead person do? None. It is only after being made alive that a person is able to believe. Faith appears in verse 8, but life appears first in verse 5. God begins his work of regeneration in dead people. He brings us to life in response to the preaching of the word, and then we are able to believe that word and are justified in the sight of God.

The new birth is not the same as believing in Jesus Christ. The new birth is having your heart changed *so that* you are able to believe. You cannot believe if you have not been born again. The new birth proceeds faith, because it is the irresistible work of God's grace — a heart transplant performed by God. God never changes our will directly; he changes what controls our will — the heart. Once the heart is changed, the will willingly comes to God.

> *"I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you."* (Ezekiel 36:26)

Only God can do that. And because now the grace of God has changed our heart, we come most willingly.

## The Comfort of God's Sovereignty in Salvation

Man, in his insignificance, cannot tell the Almighty God what to do. The idea that we can tell God "no" is a proud idea that should have no room in the church. And we should be *thankful* that we cannot resist God's saving grace — that he will save us despite ourselves. Because the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, full of grace and truth. It is that Word which draws us to him, gives us a new heart, brings us to faith, and joins us to his church for all eternity.

Praise be to God that he is sovereign — not we. That is great comfort.
